findings.summary: output_validator.py (validation layer) imports from infrastructure.project.discovery at function-level, creating a lateral dependency across unrelated subsystems
evidence: output_validator.py:200 — `from infrastructure.project.discovery import discover_projects` inside `validate_root_output_structure`
infrastructure/core/cli.py:184,284 — same deferred import pattern used for the same reason, suggesting the boundary is unclear
findings.suggestion: Pass project names as a parameter to `validate_root_output_structure` so the validation layer has no runtime dependency on the project discovery layer. Callers (cli.py, pipeline stages) already have access to discovered projects.

findings.summary: from typing import Dict/List/Tuple legacy imports persist in ~58 files across scientific/, publishing/, llm/, steganography/ while core/, validation/, and reporting/ have been migrated to builtin generics, creating a visible style island split
evidence: Grep for 'from typing import.*Dict|List|Tuple' returns 58 files — every file in scientific/, steganography/, publishing/, llm/ still uses legacy form
infrastructure/core/logging_utils.py uses `list[str]`, `dict[str, Any]` (builtin) throughout
infrastructure/reporting/pipeline_reporter.py uses `list[StageResult]`, `dict[str, Any]` (builtin) throughout
The split is not intentional variation; it reflects incomplete migration
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration: replace `Dict`, `List`, `Tuple` from `typing` with `dict`, `list`, `tuple` in all remaining files. A single sed pass or ruff rule (UP006, UP035) can enforce this.

findings.summary: audit_orchestrator.py swallows exceptions from multiple validators with only a warning log and no propagation, while sibling pipeline_reporter.py and integrity.py re-raise after logging
evidence: audit_orchestrator.py:76-78 — `except Exception as e: if verbose: logger.warning(...)` — exception silently dropped when verbose=False
audit_orchestrator.py:173-174 — `except Exception as e: logger.warning(f'Link validation failed...')` — no propagation, scan continues as if link validation succeeded
audit_orchestrator.py:195-196 — same pattern for each quality validator
pipeline_reporter.py:116-118 — `except OSError as e: logger.error(...); raise` — consistent re-raise pattern
integrity.py:69-71 — `except OSError as e: logger.error(...); integrity[...] = False` — result-based error signaling, also consistent within itself
findings.suggestion: Decide on a single strategy for recoverable validation errors: either record them in the result object (like integrity.py does) or accumulate them and raise at the end. The current per-validator silent swallow means callers cannot distinguish 'no issues found' from 'validation failed to run'.

findings.summary: output_validator.py returns False on critical missing files while pipeline_reporter.py raises OSError — inconsistent failure signaling across the same reporting subsystem
evidence: output_validator.py:113-125 — missing combined PDF sets `validation_passed = False` and returns False without raising
output_validator.py:169 — missing expected directory only logs a warning, returns True
pipeline_reporter.py:116-118 — `except OSError as e: logger.error(...); raise` — raises on write failure
pipeline_reporter.py:403-404 — `except OSError as e: logger.error(...); raise` — raises on validation markdown write failure
findings.suggestion: Standardize: validation functions that check pre-conditions (does output exist?) should consistently return a result object or bool; I/O functions that write files should consistently raise. Do not mix the two strategies in functions with similar names (`validate_*`) in the same subsystem.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py and error_aggregator.py each implement an independent markdown error-report generator with nearly identical structure, creating duplicated logic with no shared abstraction
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py:487 — `def generate_error_markdown(summary: dict[str, Any]) -> str` — free function operating on raw dict

error_aggregator.py:224 — `def _generate_markdown_report(self, summary: dict[str, Any]) -> str` — private method with identical structure: header, errors-by-type section, error-details section, 10-item truncation
Both produce a '# Error Summary' Markdown report from the same dict shape; neither calls the other
findings.suggestion: Extract a single `render_error_summary_markdown(summary: dict[str, Any]) -> str` into a shared helper (e.g., reporting/markdown_renderers.py) and have both callers delegate to it. `ErrorAggregator._generate_markdown_report` and `generate_error_markdown` become one-line delegators or are removed.

findings.summary: output_validator.py reconstructs the source PDF path using identical multi-step logic in two separate functions (validate_copied_outputs and validate_output_structure), violating DRY at the abstraction level
evidence: output_validator.py:80-107 — `validate_copied_outputs` manually reconstructs repo_root and source_project_dir from path parts to find pre-copy PDF

output_validator.py:442-472 — `validate_output_structure` contains a near-identical block: same `path_parts`, same `repo_root = output_dir.parent.parent`, same `source_pdf_dir = repo_root / 'projects' / project_name / 'output' / 'pdf'`
The two blocks differ only in minor logging strings; the core path logic is copy-pasted
findings.suggestion: Extract `_resolve_source_pdf_path(output_dir: Path, project_name: str) -> Path | None` as a shared private helper that both functions call. This also makes the fallback heuristic testable in isolation.
